author: @PetraKann
text:
It would be useful to sterilise the surface of the Face mask but the number of viruses getting through the mask will remain unchanged. 

Even N95 or N99 masks dont work at sieve filters but rather set up a maze or labyrinth for the particles to transverse which traps particles. The masks effectively filter using a statistical model not a physical model.
Even HEPA and ULPA filter types use statistical models to filter out fine particles. Although the larger particle sizes will be trapped due to the minimum gap size within the filter medium, there will always be a finite number of fine particles that will get through the filter.
Viruses are very easy to kill when outside the human body. Ordinary soap, sanitiser, bleach and even a reasonable amount of heat will kill viruses. The problem is when the virus enters the body and gets a chance to multiply in the presence of a suppressed immune system. 

Overall I wouldnt bother with copper coating a cheap dust mask which rarely fits the face snugly and has a filter medium which will not filter nano-sized particles like viruses effectively. 

Incidentally Silver is a far more effective biocidal acting metallic surface than copper - I can see why you would use copper though.
I am not against face masks provided quality masks are used that can filter most of the fine particles. F8 grade at least for this health concern.
Cheers

author: @carnivalwrestler
text:
Can you breathe through it after applying four layers of copper?  Also, in my opinion (and I am NOT a scientist or engineer or medical professional of any kind), copper is a universal biocide (although without trace elements of it in our system, we would also die, just like the poor moose calf in "Northern Exposure"). When I had a landscaping company, my horticulturist told me that you could kill a tree by pounding a few copper nails in it. Takes a while, but eventually, it dies.  Also, to prevent barnacles from collecting, paints that are used for the hulls of boats have copper in them (at least they used to, they might have found something better now). 


My point is that this might be dangerous.  I would think that if you inhale copper particles into your lungs, you would damage them. I don't know if your mask would give off fumes, but if it did, that also might damage your lungs and entire system. With those caveats, I thank you for helping us to find ways to combat this very infectious virus (and others).

author: @SaintTrinianz
text: I've been using copper infused fabric (from China)combined with a non-woven activated charcoal filter core. The combo is no doubt effective but can be a bit difficult to breathe through for more than brief periods even though I'm using the 'duckie' design. When I hit supply chain issues limiting my supply of copper cloth, I considered reviving the art of paper-making, incorporating colloidal silver and graphene. South Korea has developed and is testing a 'nano' mask. For that matter, wouldn't a very light pass over the exterior of an ordinary paper mask with the edge of a pencil provide additional protection? Sadly, I don't have any means of testing this. I also have a very fine copper mesh which is easy-breezy to breath through but probably not fine enough to provide a physical barrier against viruses. I have combined it with a layer of activated carbon though. Another note about copper face masks is that they start out quite pretty and impressive looking but the quickly oxidize upon exposure to respiration and skin oils, like a penny. I don't think this impairs it's effectiveness but it looks dirty. Not a good attribute for a reusable mask...

author: @Qalnut
text: Mate, I love the channel but this is potentially dangerous advice. The virus can live on copper for up to 4 hours and there's no reason to expect a mask or a piece of cloth treated in this manner would have any appreciable antiviral effect. The basic premise is that the pores of the mask would first have to catch virus-containing droplets prior to copper exposure, in which case it has already done its job and the copper is, at very least, totally unnecessary. In fact, I'd suspect the potential for damaging the mask or otherwise diminishing its ability to form a secure fit far outweighs any speculative, totally untested benefits. And just how much copper particulate are you going to be inhaling? Just use the masks as they are manufactured, and as directed by healthcare professionals. You've got folks all excited wanting to try this with no reason to believe it's safe or effective.

author: @WTF_BBQ
text:
I was reading some articles and studies regarding this actually.   The previous version of Corona virus (Sars) survived in surfaces for more than 24 hours,  but they only lasted around 4 hours in copper surfaces.  Other articles say that the new version of corona virus is deactivated in 1 hour of contact from copper,  but either way the anti-viral properties is only effective with longer exposure to the metal.

As far as masks are concerned,  most of the ones you can buy like the N95 only filter up to 0.3 microns.  Viral particles are 0.1 microns.  Viruses usually piggy back on droplets that are larger than 0.3 microns,  however,   droplets are about 98% water so it will eventually evaporate leaving individual viral particles to float freely.  I thought that maybe if you wear 2 layers of mask with the external layer doused with oil  (or other liquid) substance to capture virus preventing them from floating around could prove to be effective.
